nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving warm lowland rivers, swamps, and canals with murky water, dense vegetation, and muddy or sandy substrates; often found among roots and submerged wood

decorPreferences: large smooth stones and flat rocks for spawning sites
driftwood and robust root structures for shelter and territory marking
robust or artificial plants (real plants will be uprooted during spawning)
open sandy substrate areas for digging

diet.notes: Primarily carnivorous in practice. Accepts high-quality cichlid pellets, large flakes, frozen or live foods including earthworms, crickets, bloodworms, brine shrimp, krill, and feeder fish (with caution). Will also consume plant matter and algae. Varies diet with meaty and vegetable-based foods; overfeeding feeder fish risks intestinal parasites and thiamine deficiency. Feed 1–2 times daily with portions consumed in 2–3 minutes.

goodWith: other large robust Central American cichlids (convict cichlid, firemouth cichlid, green terror)
large South American cichlids (Oscar, severum) in spacious tanks
large armored catfish (plecos, hoplo catfish) as dither or bottom-dwellers
large robust barbs or tinfoil barbs in very large tanks
avoidWith: small fish and nano species (will be eaten or terrorized)
shrimp and small invertebrates (will be eaten)
other territorial cichlids of similar size without sufficient space
Electric Blue Jack Dempsey with standard Jack Dempseys (hybridization risk and aggression)

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species page image for Rocio octofasciata; typical adult male coloration showing iridescent blue-green flecking.
Wikimedia Commons image showing adult male in breeding coloration with vivid iridescent spotting.

summary: A large, boldly patterned Central American cichlid famous for its striking iridescent blue-green speckles and feisty temperament. Named after heavyweight boxer Jack Dempsey for its pugnacious nature. Best kept as a bonded pair in a large, well-decorated tank with robust tankmates.
careNotes: The Jack Dempsey Cichlid (Rocio octofasciata) is a charismatic and visually dramatic Central American cichlid native to slow, murky lowland rivers and canals from the Yucatán Peninsula in Mexico south through Guatemala, Honduras, and Belize. It was originally described as Cichlasoma octofasciatum but was reclassified in 2007 by Schmitter-Soto into the monotypic genus Rocio as Rocio octofasciata, reflecting its distinct morphological and genetic character; many older texts and trade sources still use the synonymous Cichlasoma octofasciatum or Archocentrus octofasciatus. The common name honours world heavyweight boxing champion Jack Dempsey (1895–1983) in recognition of the species' aggressive disposition. Adults reach 20–25 cm and are sexually dimorphic: males are larger and more intensely coloured with a wider array of iridescent blue-green to turquoise spots and lines; females are smaller with a more brownish base coloration and less vivid iridescence. Minimum tank size is 250 L (approximately 65 US gal) for a pair, with more spacious setups strongly recommended when housed with other cichlids. The substrate should include deep areas (≥5 cm) of sand or fine gravel to allow natural digging behaviour; flat smooth rocks or slate provide favoured spawning sites. Robust or artificial plants should be used, as live plants will be uprooted during breeding activity or territorial disputes. Water quality must be maintained with regular partial water changes (25–30% weekly); this species is sensitive to nitrate accumulation above ~40 ppm. Temperature 24–28°C is optimal, with the full tolerable range 22–30°C. pH 6.5–8.0 is broadly tolerated; moderately hard to hard water is preferred. The Electric Blue Jack Dempsey (EBJD) is a colour morph — not a separate species — characterised by reduced melanin and intensified blue iridescence; it is sometimes treated as a separate variant due to its lower vigour and slightly different care needs, but remains Rocio octofasciata. Hybridisation between standard and EBJD forms should be avoided. Avoid housing with small fish, shrimp, or docile species. Best kept as a proven bonded pair. Will reorganise tank decor extensively and are persistent diggers.
breedingNotes: The Jack Dempsey is an open substrate-spawner with strong pair bonding typical of substrate-brooding cichlids. Sexing is straightforward in adults: males are larger and more brilliantly coloured; females have a shorter dorsal fin and reduced iridescence. Conditioning with high-quality varied live and frozen foods promotes spawning readiness. A flat smooth rock, slate, or cleaned area of substrate serves as the spawning site, which the pair will vigorously clean and defend. Females deposit 500–800 adhesive eggs in neat rows; both parents provide biparental guarding of eggs and larvae. Eggs hatch in approximately 72 hours at 26°C; free-swimming fry appear within 5–7 days. Parental care continues for several weeks. Remove other tankmates during spawning as aggression toward intruders becomes extreme. Fry accept newly hatched brine shrimp nauplii and microworms from first feeding. Breeding difficulty is rated moderate due to the need for a compatible bonded pair, suitable flat spawning site, and sufficient tank volume to manage aggression; the pair bond itself is easily maintained.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Rocio octofasciata
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided taxonomy (Schmitter-Soto 2007 reclassification from Cichlasoma to Rocio), native range (Atlantic slope from Yucatán Peninsula Mexico to Honduras), adult size (males to 25 cm, females smaller), temperature 22–30°C, pH 6.5–8.0, hardness 9–20 dGH, biotope (murky slow-moving lowland rivers/canals), breeding behaviour (substrate-spawner, biparental), aggression, and tankmate guidance.

additional.name: FishBase — Rocio octofasciata
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max reported 25 cm TL males), temperature range (22–30°C), native range countries (Mexico, Guatemala, Honduras, Belize), taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes), diet classification (omnivore with strong carnivore tendency), and depth range. Confirmed synonym Cichlasoma octofasciatum and reclassification date. Also verified 'Eight-Banded Cichlid' as accepted common name in FishBase records.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Jack Dempsey Cichlid Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade care guide. Confirmed minimum tank size (55 gal / ~208 L for single, larger for pairs; 250 L chosen as conservative pairing minimum), diet recommendations (cichlid pellets, earthworms, meaty frozen foods), aggression level, tankmate guidance, and Electric Blue Jack Dempsey colour morph notes (same species, not separate).

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Jack Dempsey profile
additional.notes: Supplementary UK trade source. Confirmed reclassification into Rocio, adult size range 20–25 cm, water parameters, aggressive substrate-spawner behaviour, pair bonding, and lifespan up to 10–15 years in captivity. Notes strong pair bond and biparental fry guarding. Confirmed the Electric Blue Jack Dempsey as a colour morph, not a distinct species.

additional.name: Schmitter-Soto, J.J. (2007) — A Systematic Revision of the Genus Archocentrus, with Description of Two New Genera and Six New Species (Teleostei: Cichlidae)
additional.notes: Original taxonomic authority for Rocio octofasciata reclassification. Schmitter-Soto (2007) established the genus Rocio and placed octofasciata within it, separating it from Cichlasoma and Archocentrus based on morphological characters. Previously known as Cichlasoma octofasciatum (Regan, 1903) and Archocentrus octofasciatus in interim literature. The epithet refers to 'eight bands' (octo=eight, fasciata=banded), origin of the alternate common name 'Eight-Banded Cichlid'.

fish.breedingNotes: Open substrate-spawner with strong biparental care. Pair must be conditioned on high-quality varied foods. Female lays 500–800 adhesive eggs on cleaned flat rock or slate; eggs hatch ~72 hours at 26°C; fry free-swimming by day 5–7. Both parents guard fry aggressively for several weeks. Remove all other tankmates during breeding. Fry accept brine shrimp nauplii and microworms from first feeding.
